description:
You are acting as a security auditor who must prove that the
    system-initialisation ( “init” ) process is present and running under the
    expected PID.  
    Your assignment consists of writing **one single terminal command** (you may
    chain with “&&”, “;” or “||” but must press <ENTER> only once) that fulfils
    BOTH of the following requirements:

    1. Guarantee that a directory called 
          /home/user/audit
       exists.  
       • If the directory is already there, do nothing;  
       • If it is missing, create it.

    2. Inside that directory create (or overwrite) a file named  
          init_pid.log  
       which must contain **exactly one line** with the PID of the init
       process and **nothing else**.  
       • Format requirement (checked by an automated test):
             Line-1    1⏎
         i.e. the single character “1” followed immediately by a newline byte
         (0x0A).  
       • No spaces, extra blank lines, carriage returns or surrounding text are
         permitted.

    Hints you may (or may not) decide to use:
      – The init process inside a containerised Linux user space is always PID
        1.  
      – Utilities such as “ps”, “printf”, “echo -n”, or shell redirection “>”
        can help you capture this PID in the required format.

    Once the command has run successfully, the auditing department (the
    automated test harness) will open the file and verify that its contents are
    **exactly**:

        1\n

    Any deviation in directory path, file name, or file contents will be marked
    as a failure.
truth:
The grading script will check:

    1. That the directory /home/user/audit exists and is accessible to the
       normal user executing the script (UID 1000 inside our container).

    2. That a regular file /home/user/audit/init_pid.log exists.

    3. That the exact raw byte sequence of the file is:
           31 0A
       which corresponds to the ASCII characters
           "1\n"

    No other verification steps are required; the test does NOT evaluate the
    command history—only the final filesystem state.
name: task_000000_6666507e
